text: The surgeon begins creating a peritoneal flap, cutting from right to left using an instrument held in the right hand. Closure is performed from medial to lateral, with the medial ligament taken using energy to prevent recanalization.

The surgeon begins by identifying medial landmarks near the pubis and Cooper's ligament, without precise identification, before moving laterally while staying close to the peritoneum. The dissection occurs in the pre-transversalis plane, preparing to transition into the preparatory plane. The indirect hernia is reduced easily, and the space for mesh placement is developed.

The surgeon dissects in multiple layers, reflecting the testicular vasculature laterally while developing a space for mesh placement. The dissection then shifts medially to identify Cooper's ligament and the pubis, aiming for two centimeters of exposed pubis on the contralateral side.

The surgeon carefully dissects the areolar tissues around Cooper's ligament, ensuring there is no femoral defect. The camera orientation is adjusted to focus on parietalizing the spermatic cord structures for optimal mesh placement. The thin peritoneum is visible as the vessels are lateralized, avoiding any perforations near the external iliac vessels. The medial ligament is identified and cleanly separated from the vas deferens.

The surgeon continues to develop the lateral pocket, parietalizing the vas deferens while ensuring no additional hernias are present. Care is taken around the triangle of doom, avoiding energy or sharp dissection near the vein. Lymphatic tissue around the obturator is removed to allow the mesh to sit properly. The surgical field is well-exposed, confirming no peritoneal defect or true obturator hernia.

The surgeon exposes the midline and pubis, identifying Cooper's ligament with at least two centimeters of contralateral coverage. The direct, indirect, femoral, and obturator spaces are explored, and a cord lipoma is dissected and dropped. A large mesh is placed, with a blue line seated over Cooper's ligament and a horizontal line positioned over the iliac vessels, which are visibly pulsating behind the mesh.

The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap, starting from the lateral aspect and moving medially. The insufflation is reduced to about eight to facilitate closure. Care is taken near the epigastric vessels to avoid creating holes, and the suture line is tightened and reversed for three to four bites.
